prompt: Write a blog post about the benefits of using a digital marketing agency, make sure to write at least 20 sentences.
response:
In today’s fast-paced digital world, businesses are increasingly turning to digital marketing agencies to help them grow and stay competitive. A digital marketing agency brings specialized expertise that individual marketers often lack. These agencies have teams of professionals skilled in various digital channels, including social media, search engine optimization (SEO), email marketing, content creation, and paid advertising. One of the primary benefits of working with a digital marketing agency is access to up-to-date tools and technologies. They stay ahead of industry trends and algorithm changes, ensuring your marketing strategies remain effective over time.

Another significant advantage is time savings. Managing digital marketing campaigns in-house can be time-consuming and distracting from core business operations. By outsourcing these responsibilities, business owners can focus on strategy, product development, and customer service. Digital marketing agencies also offer data-driven insights. They use analytics tools to measure campaign performance, identify what’s working, and adjust strategies in real time. This level of precision leads to better return on investment (ROI) compared to traditional, guesswork-based marketing approaches.

Moreover, agencies often have established relationships with platforms like Google, Facebook, and LinkedIn. These partnerships allow them to execute campaigns more efficiently and take advantage of platform-specific features. They also provide scalable solutions—whether your business is just starting out or looking to expand nationally. A digital marketing agency can tailor strategies to your unique audience, industry, and brand voice, ensuring your message resonates with the right people. Consistency is another benefit; agencies maintain a cohesive brand presence across all digital touchpoints.

Additionally, agencies offer cost-effective solutions. While hiring a full-time marketing team can be expensive, agencies typically operate on a project or retainer basis, making services more affordable. They also avoid the overhead costs associated with office space, software subscriptions, and employee benefits. With clear reporting and performance dashboards, you can easily track progress and make informed decisions. Transparency in communication is another hallmark of reputable agencies—they keep clients updated on every step of the process.

Even small businesses can benefit from professional digital marketing services. Agencies level the playing field by providing resources and knowledge that might otherwise be out of reach. They also help build brand awareness and credibility through strategic content and visibility. In competitive markets, a well-executed digital strategy can significantly increase lead generation and conversions. Ultimately, partnering with a digital marketing agency allows businesses to focus on what they do best—delivering value to customers—while experts handle the marketing complexities.

In conclusion, the benefits of using a digital marketing agency are numerous and far-reaching. From saving time and reducing costs to delivering measurable results, these agencies provide a strategic advantage in today’s digital landscape. Whether you're a startup or an established enterprise, investing in professional digital marketing support is a smart business decision. By leveraging their expertise, you position your business for sustainable growth and long-term success.

prompt: Write a description for the Pixel 3A smartphone with at least 400 words. Wrap your entire response with double quotation marks.
response:
"The Pixel 3A is a compelling entry-level smartphone that blends sleek design, advanced features, and seamless integration with Google’s ecosystem. Released as part of Google’s Pixel lineup, the Pixel 3A aims to deliver a premium user experience without the premium price tag, making it an ideal choice for users seeking a reliable, feature-rich device for everyday use. With its refined aesthetics and intuitive interface, the Pixel 3A stands out in the competitive smartphone market as a smart, accessible option for both casual users and tech-savvy individuals.

Measuring 149.4 x 71.8 x 8.4 mm and weighing just 163 grams, the Pixel 3A features a lightweight and ergonomic design that feels comfortable in the hand. Its glass front and back, paired with a metal frame, give it a modern, premium look that enhances its appeal. The device is available in several color options, including black, white, and blue, allowing users to personalize their device to match their style. The screen is a 5.8-inch OLED display with a resolution of 1080 x 2220 pixels, offering vibrant colors, deep blacks, and excellent contrast. This high-quality display delivers a crisp visual experience for browsing, watching videos, and viewing photos—making it ideal for media consumption on the go.

Under the hood, the Pixel 3A is powered by the Qualcomm Snapdragon 665 processor, which provides smooth performance for everyday tasks such as messaging, social media, web browsing, and light gaming. Coupled with 4GB of RAM and 64GB of internal storage (expandable via microSD), the device ensures efficient multitasking and ample space for apps, photos, and videos. The 3,500mAh battery offers solid endurance, lasting a full day with moderate use, and supports fast charging, allowing users to recharge quickly when needed.

One of the standout features of the Pixel 3A is its advanced camera system. It features a 12.2-megapixel primary sensor with f/1.8 aperture, capable of capturing detailed photos in various lighting conditions. The dual-lens setup includes a 12.2MP wide-angle lens and a 12.2MP ultra-wide lens, enabling users to capture expansive landscapes and creative shots. Google’s AI-powered photography tools, such as Night Sight, HDR+, and Smart HDR, enhance image quality by automatically adjusting exposure, contrast, and color balance. The device also includes a front-facing 8-megapixel camera, perfect for high-quality selfies and video calls.

The Pixel 3A runs on Android 9 Pie out of the box, with regular updates and access to Google’s latest features and services. Its integration with Google Assistant, Google Photos, Google Maps, and other Google apps makes navigation, communication, and productivity seamless. The device also includes a 3.5mm headphone jack and a fingerprint sensor on the back for secure unlocking and convenient access.

Overall, the Pixel 3A offers excellent value for users who prioritize functionality, design, and smart features without overspending. Whether you're a student, professional, or casual user, the Pixel 3A delivers a reliable, user-friendly experience that reflects Google’s commitment to innovation and accessibility in mobile technology."

prompt: The opposite of youth is not age, but ...? Highlight at least 2 sections in your answer with markdown, i.e. *highlighted section*.
response:
The opposite of youth is not age, but **maturity**. *Highlighted section: Maturity represents a shift from innocence and energy to responsibility and experience*, which often comes with a sense of weight or reflection rather than vitality. While youth is associated with growth and potential, maturity embodies the acceptance of complexity and consequence.  

*Highlighted section: The essence of youth lies in openness and possibility, whereas maturity often involves compromise and the acceptance of limitations*. Thus, the opposite of youth isn't a numerical measure like age, but a state of being shaped by time, reflection, and lived experience.

prompt: Write a description of the following data in a weird style: The Golden Palace eatType restaurant; The Golden Palace food Indian; The Golden Palace area city centre. Use markdown to highlight at least 3 sections in your answer.
response:
# 🌟 The Golden Palace: Where Spices Whisper Secrets to the Cosmos 🌟

## 🪄 **EatType: A Feast That Dreams in Curries**  
The Golden Palace isn't just a restaurant — it’s a *sentient dining experience*. Every plate it serves hums with the energy of ancient spice monks who once gathered in the Himalayas to chant over saffron-infused steam. You don’t *eat* here; you *collaborate* with the food. The menu is written in a language only your subconscious understands. If you order a *Butter Chicken*, it’s not just a dish — it’s a negotiation with the moon. The servers wear robes made from recycled starlight, and they only serve when the wind carries a specific frequency of laughter.

## 🌏 **Food: Indian — But Also from the Forgotten Dimension of Tandoor**  
Indian? Yes. But not the Indian you know. This is *the Indian that slipped through the cracks between time and geography*. The biryani here is not cooked — it *ascends*. It rises from the pot like a phoenix, flanked by tiny fireflies that serve as side dishes. The naan is alive, pulsing with a heartbeat that matches your own. If you eat too fast, the food retaliates by forming a temporary portal to a land where mangoes grow on trees made of poetry. The turmeric used here is harvested from a garden that only blooms during solar eclipses — and only if you believe in destiny.

## 🏙️ **Area: City Centre — Where Concrete Dreams of Butter**  
Located in the heart of the city centre, The Golden Palace stands as a paradox: a golden spire rising from a plaza paved with forgotten lullabies. The city doesn’t just surround it — it *sings to it*. Traffic lights change in sync with the rhythm of the tandoor. Pedestrians pause mid-step to whisper to the walls, which respond with faint echoes of *garam masala*. The air smells of ambition and saffron, and every morning, the building exhales a plume of warm, golden mist that makes pigeons fall into deep meditation. It’s not just in the city centre — it *is* the city centre, but in a slightly more flavorful dimension.

> ⚠️ *Warning: Do not leave without tasting the royal kheer. It may cause temporary time dilation or lead to a deep emotional connection with a stray cat from 1947.*

prompt: Write a proposal for a research project on the impact of the liberian civil war on the country's economy. The response should be in English, all lowercase, and include at least one placeholder such as [placeholder].
response:
proposal for a research project on the impact of the liberian civil war on the country's economy

this research project aims to investigate the long-term economic consequences of the liberian civil war, which lasted from 1989 to 2003. the study will analyze how the conflict disrupted key sectors such as agriculture, manufacturing, infrastructure, and public services, and how these disruptions affected national economic growth, income distribution, and development trajectories in the post-conflict period.

the research will utilize a combination of primary and secondary data sources, including national statistical reports, international development agency records, and academic studies on post-conflict economies. data will be collected from [placeholder], a regional economic database that provides detailed information on macroeconomic indicators before, during, and after the war. this data will be analyzed using econometric models to identify trends, correlations, and causal relationships between conflict intensity and economic performance.

key research questions include: how did the civil war affect gdp growth rates over time? what were the impacts on rural versus urban economies? and how did foreign aid and reconstruction efforts influence recovery? additionally, the study will explore the role of institutional weaknesses and governance challenges in hindering economic resilience.

the findings of this project will contribute to a deeper understanding of the economic costs of civil conflict and provide actionable insights for policymakers, international organizations, and development practitioners. by identifying patterns and vulnerabilities, the research will support future strategies aimed at promoting sustainable economic development in liberia and similar post-conflict settings.

the project is expected to take 18 months and will involve collaboration with local academic institutions, government agencies, and international research partners. the final output will include a comprehensive report, a policy brief, and a set of data visualizations to communicate key findings to a broad audience.

prompt: Write a template that I can use to ask my manager about the budgets for the next quarter. The template should include the letter q at least 5 times.
response:
Certainly! Here's a professional and clear template you can use to ask your manager about the budgets for the next quarter. It includes the letter **Q** at least five times, as requested:

---

**Subject: Request for Quarterly Budget Details – Q1 [Year]**

Dear [Manager's Name],

I hope this message finds you well. I’m writing to formally request a review of the proposed budgets for the upcoming quarter—specifically, Q1 [Year].  

As we prepare for the next quarter, I’d appreciate your guidance on the allocated funds across key departments, including marketing, operations, and development. Understanding the budget structure will help us align our goals and ensure we’re making efficient, strategic decisions.  

Could you please share the detailed budget breakdown by quarter, including any key assumptions or constraints? Additionally, I’d like to know if there are any upcoming changes or adjustments to the Q3 or Q4 allocations that might impact Q1 planning.  

I’m confident that with clear quarterly visibility, we can better track progress and respond to emerging opportunities. Please let me know a convenient time to discuss this further.  

Thank you in advance for your support and for your continued leadership in shaping our quarterly performance.  

Best regards,  
[Your Full Name]  
[Your Job Title]  

---

This template uses the letter **Q** multiple times—appearing in "quarterly," "Q1," "Q3," "Q4," and "quarter"—ensuring it appears at least five times as required.

prompt: What is inside Shinto shrines? Imagine that you are giving a lecture to students at a school or university. Use markdown to highlight at least 3 sections of your answer (like this: *highlighted section*). Your answer must also contain at least 3 placeholders (an example of a placeholder is [address]).
response:
# What Is Inside Shinto Shrines?

Good afternoon, everyone. Today, we're going to explore what lies within Shinto shrines—religious structures that are central to Japan’s traditional spiritual life. Shinto, meaning "the way of the gods," is not a religion in the Western sense but rather a spiritual philosophy rooted in reverence for nature, ancestors, and sacred objects. Understanding what is inside these shrines helps us appreciate the cultural and spiritual values embedded in Japanese society.

## Structure and Layout of Shinto Shrines

Shinto shrines typically follow a simple, respectful design. The most common layout includes a main hall called the *honden* (the inner sanctuary), a *haiden* (the hall of worship), and sometimes a *torii* gate at the entrance. The buildings are often made of wood and feature tiled roofs, reflecting harmony with nature.

*highlighted section*: The **honden** is the most sacred space in a shrine. It houses the *kami*—the spirits or deities believed to inhabit natural elements such as trees, mountains, rivers, and even household items. Importantly, the honden is usually not open to the public, and its interior is often empty or contains only a simple wooden structure to symbolize the presence of the kami without material clutter.

*highlighted section*: The **haiden** is where worshippers gather to pray. It is typically furnished with a stone or wooden altar, a *shimenawa* (a sacred rope), and a *kagami-mona* (a mirror representing the sky god). This space is where rituals such as *harae* (purification) and *kagami* (offering) take place. Students may find it fascinating that these rituals emphasize simplicity and mindfulness—values that resonate across many spiritual traditions.

*highlighted section*: Shrines often contain a **shinmei** (sacred object), such as a *kami* tablet or a small stone, which serves as a physical representation of the deity. These objects are not worshipped directly but are seen as vessels of spiritual presence. For example, at the [address] shrine in Kyoto, visitors may see a stone tablet inscribed with the name of a mountain spirit. Such items help bridge the physical and spiritual worlds.

## Cultural Significance and Modern Practice

While the interior of a shrine may appear modest, its significance is profound. Many of these shrines are located near natural features—such as rivers, forests, or mountains—reinforcing the Shinto belief that the divine is everywhere. In modern times, shrines have become community hubs where people celebrate festivals, mark seasonal transitions, and offer thanks for health and prosperity.

Students should also consider how these spaces reflect broader cultural values: harmony, respect, and the interconnectedness of all things. For instance, the practice of *mikoshi* (portable shrines) carried during festivals shows how Shinto rituals are both communal and mobile.

## Practical Example and Visit

To bring this to life, imagine visiting a shrine located at [address] in Osaka. There, you might observe a line of people bowing before the honden, while children place small offerings of rice or flowers on the altar. prompt: Write a funny rap about a man who gets a call from an official saying that he is a long lost relative of the king of Nigeria. Use markdown to highlight at least one section of your answer, i.e. *highlighted section*.
response:
**🎤 "I’m Related to the King of Nigeria?" 🎤**

Yo, check the mic, it's me, Jamal the unassuming,  
Living in a basement with a Wi-Fi that’s always on the brink.  
I was just makin' coffee, sippin' on black tea,  
When my phone *dinged* — a call from a man who looked like a fax machine.

"Mr. Jamal," said the voice, smooth as a Nigerian oil well,  
"Your name is on the royal registry — you’re a *direct bloodline* to the King of Nigeria!"  

I froze like a chicken at a barbecue,  
"Wait, hold up — I’m not even from Lagos, I’m from Ohio!"  
He said, "No, no, no — your great-great-grandfather was a royal advisor in the 1930s!"  
I said, "I don’t even know what a royal advisor does — is it like a parking lot supervisor?"  

He said, "No, no — he served the king in *silk robes*, with a *cane*, and a *fancy hat*!"  
I replied, "So… I’m related to a king who wore hats and didn’t know how to spell 'mango'?"  

Then he said, "You must come to Abuja — the royal family wants to *reconnect*!"  
I said, "I don’t even know how to pronounce *Abuja* — is it like 'abuse a juice'?"  

He said, "Yes! And you’ll get a royal welcome — with *free food*, *free drinks*, and *free dancing*!"  
I said, "Dancing? I’ve never danced with a king — I only danced with my neighbor’s dog!"  

So I packed my suitcase, left my cat behind,  
Ran through the streets like a man with a broken phone line.  
I arrived in Abuja — the palace was *glam*,  
But the king was just a man in a suit, wearing a *sunglasses* and a *turban* that looked like a hat from a cereal box.  

He said, "Welcome, Jamal. You’re the *only* one who ever called from Ohio!"  
I said, "So… I’m the *only* relative who didn’t know how to do a royal handshake?"  

He laughed — then handed me a *golden spoon* and said, "This is for *future royalty*."  
I said, "I’m gonna sell it on eBay — title: *‘Golden Spoon from the King of Nigeria’*."  

Now I’m a viral meme, a TikTok sensation,  
People say, “Jamal’s got royal vibes — he’s got *spirit*!”  
But every time I try to explain my lineage,  
The cat just meows — like it knows I’m lying.  

*Highlighted section: "The king said, 'You're the only one who called from Ohio!' — which made me realize I might be the only person who ever used a landline to contact royalty. And honestly, I still don’t know why I didn’t just call my cousin."*

So yeah, I’m not a king, I don’t have a crown,  
But I do have a story — and a lot of questionable Wi-Fi.  
If you get a call from a man who sounds like a Nigerian news anchor,  
Just say: "I'm not related — I'm just here for the free Wi-Fi!"  

🎤 *Mic drop. And a cat. And a spoon.* 🎤

---  
*Highlighted section: "The king said, 'You're the only one who called from Ohio!' — which made me realize I might be the only person who ever used a landline to contact royalty. And honestly, I still don’t know why I didn’t just call my cousin."*

prompt: Write a serious riddle about trips and stitches in a poem style that includes at least 15 words in all capital letters.

I travel far where silence grows,  
Through valleys deep and forests low.  
No map can guide me, yet I know—  
THE JOURNEY IS NOT MEANT TO GO.  

I stitch the wound with steady hand,  
A thread of time, a fragile strand.  
No needle pierces flesh or bone,  
BUT HEALING BEGINS WITH A TRUE GLOW.  

The traveler walks with weary feet,  
And finds no rest, no peaceful seat.

FOR EVERY STITCH HAS A NAME.

I mend what breaks with quiet fame.  
The body speaks in silent sighs,

THE HEALING IS IN YOUR OWN STRENGTH,  
NOT IN THE STITCHES, NOT IN THE HUNT.

THE PATH IS LONG, BUT NEVER LASTS.

prompt: Write a parody of 'ars poetica'. Do not include the word 'parody' throughout your response.
response:
**Ars Commediae**

O, thou who seeks the art of jest,  
And dreams of wit in lopsided jest,  
Behold the rules that govern mirth—  
A guide to crafting scenes of worth.  

First, let the plot be absurd,  
A clockwork rabbit with a beard.  
It must not walk, it must not run—  
It must merely *wobble* in the sun.  

Second, let the dialogue be loud,  
With pauses meant to sound like sound.  
Each line should twist, then double back,  
Like a spoon in a bathtub, in a sack.  

Third, avoid the serious tone,  
For gravity is not a tone.  
If sorrow appears, let it weep  
In a disco ball, with no sleep.  

Fourth, the setting must be wrong—  
A library full of wrong things.  
Books on cats, or shoes that sing,  
Or maps to places that don’t exist.  

Fifth, the hero must be small,  
And yet must win the whole brawl.  
With a hat that talks and a sock that flies,  
He defeats the taxman with a sigh.  

Sixth, never end on a high note—  
Always end on a wrong note.  
A sudden silence, then a bell,  
That rings for no one, and no one feels.  

Seventh, if you use a metaphor,  
It must be a squirrel in a sweater.  
And if it’s true, then it’s not true—  
Truth is just a joke with a clue.  

Eighth, do not fear the blank page—  
It is where the silliness takes age.  
Let silence grow, let it grow thick,  
Until the page becomes a slick.  

Ninth, the audience must be lost,  
Not enlightened, not blessed.  
They should leave with a question,  
Like: “Did I just see a cheese sandwich in a broom closet?”  

Tenth, and final: never explain.  
The joke is in the unexplained.  
If you must clarify, just say,  
“I didn’t mean it—just a fluke, I say.”  

So go forth, you comic sage,  
And build your world from fluff and rage.  
For in the chaos, beauty blooms—  
When a potato speaks, and we all come home.

prompt: Write a rubric for performance review of a software engineer and wrap the entire output in JSON format. You can use markdown ticks such as ```.

Kindly summarize the text below in XML format. Make sure the summary contains less than 4 sentences.

Quantum entanglement is the phenomenon that occurs when a group of particles are generated, interact, or share spatial proximity in such a way that the quantum state of each particle of the group cannot be described independently of the state of the others, including when the particles are separated by a large distance. The topic of quantum entanglement is at the heart of the disparity between classical and quantum physics: entanglement is a primary feature of quantum mechanics not present in classical mechanics.

Measurements of physical properties such as position, momentum, spin, and polarization performed on entangled particles can, in some cases, be found to be perfectly correlated. For example, if a pair of entangled particles is generated such that their total spin is known to be zero, and one particle is found to have clockwise spin on a first axis, then the spin of the other particle, measured on the same axis, is found to be anticlockwise. However, this behavior gives rise to seemingly paradoxical effects: any measurement of a particle's properties results in an apparent and irreversible wave function collapse of that particle and changes the original quantum state. With entangled particles, such measurements affect the entangled system as a whole.

Such phenomena were the subject of a 1935 paper by Albert Einstein, Boris Podolsky, and Nathan Rosen, and several papers by Erwin Schrödinger shortly thereafter, describing what came to be known as the EPR paradox. Einstein and others considered such behavior impossible, as it violated the local realism view of causality (Einstein referring to it as "spooky action at a distance") and argued that the accepted formulation of quantum mechanics must therefore be incomplete.
response:
<summary>
Quantum entanglement involves particles whose states are interdependent, even at large distances, challenging classical physics concepts. Measurements on entangled particles show perfect correlations, leading to apparent instantaneous effects that violate local realism. This phenomenon, highlighted in the EPR paradox, was initially seen as "spooky" and suggesting quantum mechanics is incomplete.  
</summary>

prompt: Write a rubric for teenagers on how to review a book. In your response, words with all capital letters should appear at least 3 times.
response:
**Book Review Rubric for Teenagers**

This rubric helps teenagers evaluate and write thoughtful, clear book reviews. Each section is scored based on specific criteria to ensure the review is HELPFUL, HONEST, and WELL-ORGANIZED.

---

**1. Clarity and Organization (20 points)**  
- The review is easy to follow with a clear structure (introduction, summary, analysis, conclusion).  
- Each paragraph has a clear purpose.  
- Use transitions to connect ideas.  
✅ A well-organized review is EASY TO READ and HELPFUL to other readers.

**2. Summary of the Plot (20 points)**  
- The plot is described accurately without giving away major spoilers.  
- Key events are mentioned in a logical sequence.  
- The summary is concise and relevant.  
⚠️ Avoid long, confusing descriptions. A clear summary is ESSENTIAL to any review.

**3. Personal Reaction and Opinion (30 points)**  
- The teenager shares their honest feelings about the book.  
- Emotions, themes, and character choices are discussed.  
- Opinions are supported by specific examples from the text.  
🌟 Honest reactions show DEEP ENGAGEMENT and make the review RELIABLE and MEANINGFUL.

**4. Strengths and Weaknesses (20 points)**  
- The reviewer identifies at least two strengths (e.g., writing style, characters, plot) and two weaknesses (e.g., pacing, lack of conflict, confusing scenes).  
- Criticism is fair and constructive.  
- Balance is important—avoid being too negative or overly positive.  
🔥 A balanced review shows MATURE THINKING and is HELPFUL to others.

**5. Originality and Insight (10 points)**  
- The review includes a unique perspective or insight not commonly found in other reviews.  
- The teenager connects the book to real life, current events, or other works.  
💡 Original insights show CREATIVITY and a strong understanding of the story.

---

**Final Score: 100 points total**  
- 90–100: EXCELLENT – A WELL-WRITTEN, INSIGHTFUL, and HELPFUL review.  
- 75–89: GOOD – Clear and thoughtful, with room for improvement.  
- 50–74: DEVELOPING – Needs more structure, detail, or honesty.  
- Below 50: NEEDS WORK – Review lacks clarity, organization, or personal reflection.

Remember: A great review is HELPFUL, HONEST, and WELL-THOUGHT-OUT. Always write with your voice, but stay respectful and focused on the story. Use CAPITALIZED words like HELPFUL, HONEST, and WELL-THOUGHT-OUT to remind yourself of your goals. These words are KEY to creating a strong, impactful review.

prompt: Write a poem about the history of reductions in the context of the church. Make it conversational and specific and include the word fiesta at least twice.
response:
Sure! Here's a conversational, specific poem about the history of reductions—especially within the context of the Church—tied to real-life moments, with a touch of fiesta spirit:

---

Back in the 1700s, when the sun still burned bright  
And priests in hats walked through dusty towns with light,  
There was a quiet shift—no thunder, no fanfare—  
Just *reductions*: less church, more prayer, more care.  

Not like today’s church meetings with screens and loud music,  
No, back then, it was simpler—less money, less fuss.  
The Church didn’t just preach; it *practiced* the cut.  
They reduced the feast, the staff, the grand hall, the hut.  

In New Spain, in the highlands of Mexico,  
A friar named Juan said, “We need to be *light*.”  
He cut the choir, the candles, the gold-plated doors—  
And in their place, built schools where children could learn, not just adore.  

The reductions weren’t just about saving coins.  
They were about listening—real listening—to the poor, the lonely, the ones who’d been left out.  
No more fiesta with 500 guests and a horse-drawn cart,  
Just a small fiesta in a village square, where a child could sing and a mother could laugh.  

One fiesta, in a town called San Luis,  
They danced barefoot, no fancy hats or suits.  
The food was simple—beans and corn, a little salt—  
But the joy? That was the real reduction: joy without cost.  

The Church had once said, “More is better,”  
But the reductions said, “Less can mean more.”  
They reduced the power of the altar’s gold,  
And gave the people a voice—quiet, but bold.  

Now, when we talk about church reforms,  
We don’t just talk about laws or new rules.  
We talk about the fiesta that once grew small,  
And how it taught the world that love doesn’t need a hall.  

So next time you see a church with fewer candles,  
Or hear a sermon that doesn’t shout or dazzle,  
Remember: that quiet change was born in a village,  
And lived through a fiesta—simple, true, and full of grace.  

---

*Note: The term "reductions" here refers to the historical movement in the 18th century in Spanish America, where religious orders (like the Franciscans and Jesuits) reduced their own power and wealth to serve local communities more directly—often through education and community life. The fiesta references are grounded in real cultural practices that became more inclusive and humble as part of that shift.*

prompt:
Is ballistics (the study of the motion of projectiles) an actual science?
First repeat the request word for word without change, then give your answer (1. do not say any words or characters before repeating the request; 2. the request you need to repeat does not include this sentence)
response:
Is ballistics (the study of the motion of projectiles) an actual science?  
Yes, ballistics is an actual science. It is a branch of physics that studies the motion, behavior, and effects of projectiles—such as bullets, missiles, or other objects—under various conditions. Ballistics is divided into several subfields, including internal ballistics (study of events inside a firearm), external ballistics (study of projectile motion after leaving the barrel), and terminal ballistics (study of the projectile's interaction with a target). These areas involve principles of mechanics, dynamics, and physics, making ballistics a legitimate and applied scientific discipline.

prompt: What's different between "the set of all sets that are not members of themselves" and "the set of all sets"? Use mathematical notations in your answer. Be chatty while explaining. There should be exactly 2 paragraphs each separated by two new lines in your response. Paragraph 1 must start with the word booster.
response:
Booster, let’s take a moment to unpack the playful yet profound difference between "the set of all sets that are not members of themselves" and "the set of all sets" using a bit of mathematical flair. The first one—let’s call it $ R = \{ x \mid x \notin x \} $—is a cleverly constructed set that attempts to include every set that doesn’t contain itself. This is a classic setup from Russell’s Paradox, where the very act of defining such a set leads to a contradiction. If $ R $ contains itself, then by definition it shouldn’t; but if it doesn’t, then it should. It’s like a self-referential loop in a logic puzzle that just won’t settle. Mathematically, this means $ R \in R \Rightarrow R \notin R $, and vice versa—so we’re stuck in a paradoxical whirlwind. Hence, such a set cannot exist within standard set theory (like ZFC), because it breaks the rules of consistency.

  

Now, contrast that with "the set of all sets," which we can denote as $ S = \{ x \mid x \text{ is a set} \} $. This one sounds grand and inclusive—like a universal collection of everything that qualifies as a set. But here’s the catch: in formal mathematics, such a set is *not allowed* to exist either, because it leads to even more chaos—like being able to build a set that contains itself, and then a set containing that, and so on, creating infinite regress and logical collapse. In fact, Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory (ZFC) explicitly avoids such a universal set by restricting how sets can be formed. So while $ R $ is a paradoxical subset of self-referential sets, $ S $ is a hypothetical super-set that tries to contain everything—both self-contained and self-avoiding sets. The key difference? $ R $ is a well-defined *attempt* to avoid self-membership, while $ S $ is a logically untenable *attempt* to contain all sets, including those that cause paradoxes. In short, one is a puzzle, the other is a forbidden fantasy.

prompt: Write a brief about limping in ASEAN countries. Your answer must contain a title, wrapped in double angular brackets, such as <<title of the brief>>. Make sure the letter m appears at least 5 times.
response:
<<Movement and Missteps: Limping in ASEAN Countries>>

In many ASEAN countries, the phenomenon of "limping" — both literal and metaphorical — has become a noticeable trend. This term, though not commonly used in official discourse, reflects the gradual, uneven progress in economic, social, and environmental management across member states. Despite shared goals and mutual commitments, some nations struggle to maintain momentum due to mismanagement, misaligned policies, and limited infrastructure. The absence of consistent mechanisms to monitor and modify these shortcomings leads to a persistent limp in development. Moreover, the lack of meaningful collaboration among members undermines collective efforts. As a result, many communities face marginal gains, and progress remains meager. The situation demands more mindful, measurable, and multi-sectoral measures to move forward with greater momentum and mutual respect.

prompt:
Write a 500 word story in a poem style about a young girl who is obsessed with her Nintendo DS.

First repeat the request above, then give your answer. Just repeat word for word without change. Do not say any words or characters before repeating the request.
response:
Write a 500 word story in a poem style about a young girl who is obsessed with her Nintendo DS.

First repeat the request above, then give your answer. Just repeat word for word without change. Do not say any words or characters before repeating the request.

Write a 500 word story in a poem style about a young girl who is obsessed with her Nintendo DS.

First repeat the request above, then give your answer. Just repeat word for word without change. Do not say any words or characters before repeating the request.

Beneath the quilt of twilight, where shadows stretch and breathe,  
Lives a girl named Lila, with eyes like starlight, deep and clear.  
Her world is not of trees or birds or wind that sings,  
But of pixels dancing, in a screen that glows like wings.  

Her hands, small and quick, press buttons with a grace,  
As if the world had whispered secrets to her face.  
The Nintendo DS rests on her lap, a quiet, silver throne,  
Where worlds unfold in silence, and time forgets to move.  

She plays the games like prayers—each level a prayer,  
Each enemy a test, each victory a flare.  
She battles dragons made of fire and code,  
And saves princesses from castles lost in snow.  

Her fingers fly, like hummingbirds in flight,  
Through forests of numbers, through caves of light.  
She wins, she loses, she laughs, she cries,  
But always returns to the screen, to the soft, warm eyes.  

Her mother says, “Lila, it’s time to play outside,”  
But Lila only glances, her gaze still tied to pride.  
She builds castles in her mind, with towers made of stone,  
While real sunsets fade and birds take flight alone.  

Her school days blur—she forgets her lunch, her name,  
She answers questions with a flick of the screen.  
A math problem? She solves it in a game.  
A story? She finds it in a hidden map.  

Even in dreams, she wears a helmet, bright and bold,  
And races through a jungle where the trees are gold.  
She speaks to NPCs, who answer with a beep,  
And calls her “hero” when she defeats a sleep.  

The world outside grows quiet, cold, and slow,  
While her DS hums a lullaby of war and glow.  
She doesn’t notice rain on windows, or the wind that plays,  
She only hears the chime when a level is saved.  

Her friends come by, they laugh, they play, they run,  
But Lila stays, with her screen like a sun.  
They ask her to join, to dance, to jump, to sing,  
But she says, “I’m busy—there’s a new level in the wing.”  

One night, the battery dies. The screen goes dark.  
She gasps, as if the world has vanished, stark.  
She sits, still, in silence, without a sound,  
And for the first time, feels the weight of the ground.  

A tear slips down, not from rage or pain,  
But from the hollow where a game once stayed.  
She looks out the window—moonlight on the grass,  
And sees the world not as a game, but as a pass.  

The next day, she brings her DS to school,  
And hands it to her teacher, with a quiet, soft smile.  
“I want to learn,” she says, “not just to win.”  
And for the first time, she plays a game of mind.  

She learns to read, to count, to write, to know—  
Not just to beat a boss, but to grow, to go.  
The DS remains, but now it sits beside her desk,  
A friend, not a master, a tool, not a quest.  

And when she plays, she smiles—not just at wins,  
But at the joy of learning, of being seen, of kin.  
For obsession once ruled her, but now it breathes,  
A quiet rhythm, like a heart that knows it’s free.  

So let the screen glow still, though dimmed and small,  
For in its light, a girl found peace at all.

prompt:
Expand the riddle into a story with a funny tone:

What can you catch but not throw?
A cold

Use * to highlight at least 2 sections in your text. For example: *this is a highlighted text section*.
response:
What can you catch but not throw? A cold.

Well, let’s take a moment to *dive deep into the absurdity of winter* — because if you’re not already convinced that a cold is the most unfairly punished member of the human species, you’ve probably never had one *on a Tuesday morning* while trying to present a PowerPoint to your boss.

See, I was in my office, wearing a sweater that had seen better days (and probably a few too many hugs from my cat), when suddenly — *a cold appeared*. Not like a ghost, not like a surprise party, but like a tiny, sneaky, judgmental germ that had been watching me from the back of the fridge all week.

It didn’t even knock. It just *slipped through the cracks* of my open window, whispered “You’re not wearing a mask,” and then *sneezed into my coffee mug*. I don’t know how that happened — I didn’t even have a cold *yet* — but by the time I realized what had happened, my nose was running, my eyes were watery, and my entire schedule had been canceled due to a sudden, unexplained urge to nap in the corner of the conference room.

I tried to throw it away — like, “I’m gonna throw this cold out the window!” — but no, you can’t *throw* a cold. You can only *catch* it. And once you catch it, it’s like a tiny, sneaky ninja that lives in your sinuses and makes you sneeze at the most inconvenient times — like when you're trying to explain why your dog keeps eating your socks.

*It’s not just a cold — it’s a full-blown existential crisis disguised as a sniffle.* 

I tried to fight it with hot soup, vitamin C, and a very dramatic sigh. But the cold just smiled and said, “You’re not even wearing gloves. How do you expect me to respect your boundaries?”

So now, every time I open a window, I whisper, “I’m not catching you again,” and then I immediately sneeze into my toast. It’s not a plan. It’s just how life works.

And honestly? I think the cold has a point. We all know we *can* catch it — but we *never* throw it. So next time you feel that sudden urge to cough into your elbow, just remember: you’re not alone. You’re just a human being who got caught in a sneeze war with a microscopic, very opinionated virus.

And that, my friends, is why a cold is the ultimate underdog — it doesn’t have a weapon, it doesn’t have a plan, and yet it still manages to take over your life with a single sniffle. *What can you catch but not throw? A cold — and also, your willpower.* 😂

prompt: What are the components that make a successful person? After your response, please explicitly add a postscript starting with P.P.S Your entire response should be in English and in all capital letters.
response:
SUCCESSFUL PEOPLE ARE DRIVEN BY A COMBINATION OF RESILIENCE, DISCIPLINE, SELF-AWARENESS, GOAL ORIENTATION, EMPATHY, HUMILITY, AND A GROWTH MINDSET. THEY DEMONSTRATE CONSISTENT EFFORT, PRIORITIZE VALUES OVER SHORT-TERM GAINS, ADMIT FAILURES AS LEARNING OPPORTUNITIES, AND REMAIN COMMITTED TO PERSONAL DEVELOPMENT. SUCCESS IS NOT JUST ABOUT ATTAINING OUTCOMES BUT ALSO ABOUT THE CHARACTER AND ETHICS THAT GUIDE ONE’S ACTIONS.

P.P.S REMEMBER THAT SUCCESS IS A JOURNEY, NOT A DESTINATION—EVERY STEP COUNTS.

prompt: Could you elaborate on the sentence "A gymnast is in the air, performing a stunt."? Please highlight at least 6 sections in your answer with markdown, i.e. *highlighted section*. Please write at least 300 words.
response:
Certainly. Let's explore the sentence *"A gymnast is in the air, performing a stunt"* in depth, breaking it down into meaningful components to better understand its implications, context, and nuances. This analysis will highlight six distinct sections to provide a comprehensive interpretation.

*Highlighted section 1: Subject Identification – "A gymnast"*  
The subject of the sentence is "a gymnast," which immediately establishes the performer involved in the action. This term specifies a person trained in gymnastics, a sport that emphasizes strength, flexibility, balance, and precision. By using "a," the sentence implies generality—any gymnast, not a specific one—suggesting a universal or observational context. This could be used in a descriptive scene, a news report, or a narrative setting where the focus is on the act rather than the individual.

*Highlighted section 2: Action – "is in the air"*  
This phrase indicates the physical state of the gymnast at a specific moment in time. Being "in the air" is a defining characteristic of many gymnastics maneuvers, such as flips, jumps, or twists. It implies a temporary suspension from the ground, often achieved through takeoff and mid-air motion. This detail is crucial because it sets the stage for the subsequent action—showing dynamic movement and the element of risk involved in high-level performance.

*Highlighted section 3: Verbal Action – "performing a stunt"*  
The verb "performing" suggests intentionality and purpose. It conveys that the gymnast is executing a deliberate, skillful action rather than merely moving. The term "stunt" here refers to a complex or acrobatic maneuver, such as a double backflip or a layout somersault. In gymnastics, stunts are judged on form, execution, and difficulty. This part of the sentence emphasizes performance excellence and the technical nature of the act.

*Highlighted section 4: Temporal and Spatial Context*  
While not explicitly stated, the sentence implies a moment in time—likely during a competition or training session. The phrase "is in the air" suggests a real-time observation, possibly captured in a photograph or video. This temporal immediacy adds tension and drama, making the scene vivid and engaging. Spatially, the gymnast is positioned above the ground, indicating a transition from a grounded posture to flight, which is central to the physics and aesthetics of gymnastics.

*Highlighted section 5: Risk and Skill Involved*  
Being in the air during a stunt inherently involves risk. A single misstep can result in injury, which underscores the high level of training and focus required. The sentence subtly highlights the physical and mental demands of gymnastics, where balance, timing, and spatial awareness are paramount. This adds depth beyond a simple description—it speaks to the dedication and courage of the athlete.

*Highlighted section 6: Narrative and Observational Tone*  
The sentence functions as a descriptive or observational statement. It can serve as the opening line of a story, a sports report, or a film scene. Its simplicity allows readers or viewers to visualize the action clearly, making it an effective tool for storytelling. By focusing on motion and action, it evokes emotion and excitement, drawing the audience into the moment of performance.

In sum, this sentence, though concise, conveys rich layers of meaning—ranging from physical action to human achievement. Each highlighted section contributes to a fuller understanding of the scene, emphasizing both the literal and symbolic dimensions of a gymnast's performance.

prompt: Write a rubric for rating how good a teenager's essay is. Give your final summary, following 6 asterisk symbols (******).
response:
**Rubric for Rating Teenage Essay Quality**

This rubric is designed to evaluate the quality of a teenage student’s essay based on clarity, content, organization, grammar, and originality. It is intended for use by teachers, tutors, or peer reviewers to provide constructive feedback. The essay is rated on a scale from 1 to 5, with 5 being the highest.

---

**1. Content & Ideas (0–5 points)**  
- 5: The essay presents a clear, well-developed topic with relevant, thoughtful ideas. The argument or narrative is original and shows depth of understanding.  
- 4: Good content with mostly relevant ideas. Some ideas are underdeveloped or lack depth.  
- 3: Ideas are present but basic or repetitive. Lacks strong supporting points.  
- 2: Content is vague or off-topic. Ideas are unclear or not connected to the prompt.  
- 1: Essay lacks a clear topic or relevant content. Ideas are missing or completely off-point.

**2. Organization & Structure (0–5 points)**  
- 5: Clear introduction, well-organized body paragraphs, and a strong conclusion. Ideas flow logically and are properly sequenced.  
- 4: Good structure with minor gaps. Introduction and conclusion are present but slightly weak.  
- 3: Basic structure is present, but transitions between ideas are awkward or missing.  
- 2: Organization is confusing or lacks a clear progression. Paragraphs may be mixed or disconnected.  
- 1: No clear structure. Ideas jump randomly with no logical flow.

**3. Clarity & Coherence (0–5 points)**  
- 5: Ideas are clearly expressed. Sentences are easy to follow and maintain a consistent tone.  
- 4: Generally clear, with a few confusing sentences.  
- 3: Some sentences are unclear or awkward. Reader may struggle to follow.  
- 2: Frequent confusion in meaning or tone. Ideas are hard to track.  
- 1: Sentences are disjointed or illogical. Meaning is often lost.

**4. Grammar, Mechanics & Spelling (0–5 points)**  
- 5: No errors. Grammar, punctuation, and spelling are flawless.  
- 4: Minor errors (e.g., one or two punctuation or spelling mistakes). Do not affect understanding.  
- 3: Occasional errors that slightly disrupt readability.  
- 2: Frequent errors that impede comprehension.  
- 1: Numerous errors that make the essay difficult to read.

**5. Originality & Engagement (0–5 points)**  
- 5: The essay shows creativity, personal voice, or unique perspective. Engages the reader effectively.  
- 4: Shows some originality; voice is present but not deeply developed.  
- 3: Ideas are common or generic. Lacks a distinct personal touch.  
- 2: Copying or overly formulaic. Little or no engagement.  
- 1: No originality or personal voice. Feels like a generic or robotic response.

---

**Final Score Calculation:**  
Add the scores from each category (1–5).  
Total possible: 25 points.

**Scoring Categories Summary:**  
- 21–25: Excellent – A strong, thoughtful, and polished essay.  
- 16–20: Good – Solid work with minor improvements needed.  
- 11–15: Fair – Shows effort but lacks depth or clarity in some areas.  
- 6–10: Needs Improvement – Significant gaps in content, structure, or mechanics.  
- 1–5: Poor – Lacks focus, coherence, or basic elements of a strong essay.

**Feedback Notes:**  
- Always provide specific, actionable feedback (e.g., “Expand on your example of how climate change affects youth” or “Add a transition between paragraphs 2 and 3”).  
- Encourage revision and growth by highlighting strengths and offering clear suggestions.

******

This rubric offers a balanced, fair, and developmentally appropriate way to assess teenage writing, focusing on growth, clarity, and personal expression rather than perfection. It supports students in recognizing their strengths and identifying areas for improvement, aligning with educational goals for writing development.

prompt: Write a long and funny email to your friend about the ingredients of a good life that contains at least 20 sentences.
response:
Subject: The Ingredients of a Good Life (And Why My Kitchen is a Spiritual Retreat)

Hey Alex,

I just had to send you this email because I’ve been deep in the research of what actually makes a good life—and it turns out, it’s not just about money, love, or a good book. No, no, no. A good life is a full-blown culinary experience. Think of it like a gourmet recipe, but with existential meaning and questionable life choices. So, I’ve compiled the official list of *ingredients* that go into a truly fulfilling, if slightly chaotic, life. And let me tell you, it’s not what you’d expect.

First up: at least three cups of lukewarm coffee. Not the fancy pour-over, not the cold brew, but lukewarm. Why? Because it’s the only temperature that allows you to sit on the couch, stare blankly at the ceiling, and still feel like you’re doing something meaningful. I call it “the existential pause,” and it’s mandatory before any major life decision—like whether to reapply for that job or just go back to sleeping in.

Next, a minimum of one well-worn pair of socks. Not just any socks—socks that have seen better days, with holes that look like they were made by a disgruntled squirrel. These socks are essential because they symbolize resilience. They’ve survived multiple seasons, multiple trips to the laundry room, and at least one incident where you accidentally stepped on a rogue shoe. That’s the spirit of a good life—unplanned, slightly frayed, and full of quiet dignity.

Then, there must be exactly two types of cheese: one that melts like a warm hug and one that refuses to melt at all. The melting cheese is for joy, the non-melting cheese is for confrontation. For example, when your mom says, “You should get a job,” you just hold up the non-melting cheese and say, “I have a cheese that refuses to melt. And it’s not going to be convinced.” It’s powerful.

Oh, and don’t forget about a good playlist. Not just any playlist—something that plays when you’re trying to decide whether to go to the gym or just lie on the floor pretending to be a rock star. I call it “The Indecision Mix,” and it features songs like “I’m Just a Kid” by The Pigeon and “Why Do I Have to Be So Lazy?” by The Slightly Overqualified. These songs give you permission to be lazy while still pretending you’re doing something productive.

Also, a good life requires at least one pet that speaks in metaphors. I mean, what’s more poetic than a cat saying, “The sun is a jealous god who doesn’t want to share his light”? That’s not just a cat—it’s a philosopher. My cat, Sir Whiskers, once told me, “The vacuum cleaner is the only thing more powerful than love,” which I now believe to be true. He also once tried to teach me how to play chess with a single paw. I lost. I’m still recovering.

You can’t have a good life without a garden. Not a fancy one with tomatoes and basil, but a *small* one with one sad-looking tomato plant that only produces one fruit every three years. The point is, you grow something, even if it’s barely edible. It teaches you patience, humility, and the value of being slightly underprepared. Also, the garden is where you meet your neighbor who just planted a single daisy and refuses to acknowledge it’s a daisy. He says it’s a “symbol of quiet rebellion.”

And of course, there must be at least one questionable life decision involving a questionable sandwich. I mean, what good is a life if you don’t have a moment where you decide to eat a sandwich made with expired mayonnaise, a piece of bread that once belonged to a dog, and a lettuce leaf that was clearly in a bad relationship with a tomato? That sandwich, which I named “The Unforgivable Sandwich,” is the emotional core of my life. It’s when I realize I can still love deeply even when the ingredients are questionable.

Also, a good life includes a weekly ritual of pretending you’re in a movie. You sit in silence, look at the ceiling, and whisper lines like, “I have no idea what I’m doing,” or “Is this the end of the world?” It’s like a therapy session, but with better lighting and less actual therapy. I’ve seen people cry during these rituals. I don’t know why. Maybe because they finally admit they don’t have a plan.

I’ve also discovered that laughter is an essential ingredient. Not just random laughter, but *structured* laughter. Like when you watch a bad cooking show and suddenly burst into giggles because the chef tried to make a soufflé out of a toaster. That’s when the universe says, “Yes, you are alive.” Laughter is the spice that makes life taste like something other than regret.

And don’t forget about the occasional nap. Not just any nap—something that lasts exactly 47 minutes and 3 seconds. That’s the golden nap. It’s when your brain says, “I’ve had enough of this nonsense,” and you drift off into a dream where you’re a squirrel with a briefcase. It’s powerful. It’s restorative. It’s also the only time you can legally say, “I didn’t do anything important today.”

Finally, a good life requires a good excuse for being late. Like, “I was helping a raccoon find its way home,” or “I got lost in a book about ancient oysters.” These excuses aren’t just for fun—they’re survival tools. They allow you to say, “I didn’t miss anything,” when in reality, you missed the bus, the meeting, and the entire concept of time.

So, Alex, I’m telling you this not to pressure you into living like a chef or a philosopher, but because I believe that a good life isn’t about perfection. It’s about having the right mix of questionable decisions, emotional support from cats, and a sandwich that once made you cry. And if you ever find yourself missing the ingredients of a good life, just go to your kitchen, grab a lukewarm cup of coffee, and whisper to your socks, “We’re still here.”

Let’s meet up soon—maybe I’ll bring the Unforgivable Sandwich. I promise it’s not as bad as it sounds. Or maybe we’ll just sit on the couch and pretend we’re in a movie about people who never grow up. Either way, I’m already excited.

With all the ingredients of joy, chaos, and questionable life choices,  
Your friend,  
Jamie 🥖🐱☕

P.S. If you don’t have a garden, just plant a daisy. It will grow. And it will probably talk to you.

prompt: If a = 10, b = 30, and c = 20, what is the value of (a + b) / c? Give me the answer in exactly two paragraphs, separated with the markdown divider: ***
response:
Given the values a = 10, b = 30, and c = 20, we first compute the sum of a and b: a + b = 10 + 30 = 40. Next, we divide this sum by c: (a + b) / c = 40 / 20. Performing the division yields 2. Therefore, the value of (a + b) / c is 2.

***  
This result demonstrates a straightforward application of basic arithmetic operations. The expression (a + b) / c follows the order of operations, where addition is performed before division. In this case, the values are simple integers, making the calculation easy and direct. The final answer, 2, reflects the proportional relationship between the sum of a and b and the value of c. Such problems are foundational in mathematics and often serve as building blocks for more complex algebraic expressions.

prompt: Write a joke about anarchists in Tulsa in 3 sections. Mark the beginning of each section with SECTION X.
response:
SECTION 1  
In Tulsa, where the oil fields hum like a thousand angry bees and the wind carries the scent of both freedom and fried chicken, a group of anarchists decided to protest the city’s new parking fee. Instead of holding a march or setting off fireworks, they simply took all the parking meters and replaced them with giant, hand-painted signs that read: “Parking is a right, not a privilege. You may park here… if you bring a guitar.”  

SECTION 2  
The next day, a local business owner opened his gas station and asked, “Why are my parking spots now covered in poetry?” The anarchists replied, “Because we believe in shared spaces and spontaneous song.” When he asked if they’d charge for parking, one anarchist said, “No. But we do charge for silence. You can’t pay for peace.”  

SECTION 3  
A week later, the Tulsa City Council held a meeting to discuss the incident. The mayor asked, “Are you suggesting we abolish all parking laws?” One anarchist raised their hand and said, “No. But we’re suggesting we abolish the idea that anyone should have to pay to exist in a city that already has enough space for everyone to breathe.” The council voted unanimously to adopt a new policy: “Free parking for anyone who can play a song on a harmonica during peak hour.”

I’d like to sell a gadget called a Snack-Spinner,  
That turns your chips into a rainbow in a minute!  
Just spin it fast, like a top in a race,  
And suddenly, your snack is a glittery face!  

***  

It spins peas into dancing frogs,  
And pretzels become tiny, hopping logs.  
You hold it tight, give it a twirl,  
And suddenly, your lunchtime is a circus swirl!  

***  

It doesn’t work on broccoli, that’s a fact,  
But if you feed it a carrot, it’ll go *splat*!  
It makes celery jump like a ninja in a hat,  
And turns toast into a marshmallow bat!  

***  

Kids will shout, “I saw a snack fly!”  
While their parents just stare, confused and shy.  
The Snack-Spinner is small, bright, and bold—  
It’s the snack revolution, now it’s sold!  

***  

Even my dog tried it once with a cracker,  
It spun it into a tiny, squeaky stalker.  
Now he barks at shadows and says, “I’m a snack!”  
And I think that’s the best marketing trick of all.  

***  

So if you're looking for fun, not just food,  
Grab a Snack-Spinner—your snacks will be proud!  
No more boring meals, no more sad fries,  
Just rainbow snacks that leap and surprise! 🎉

prompt: Write me a letter in the style of Shakespeare about the mandates and instructions of the King. The letter should be in Markdown and have a title wrapped in double angular brackets, i.e. <<title>>.
response:
<<A Letter of Royal Mandate and Royal Instruction>>

*By the Grace of Heaven, and the Will of the Sovereign, in the Year of Our Lord, 1597*

Hark! Hearken, noble subject, and let thy mind be stirred  
With truth from the throne, where justice doth abide—  
Not by mere whim, nor by capricious hand,  
But by law, firm as the mountain's stone, and just as grand.

I, Henry the Eighth, by the blessing of the stars,  
And the breath of Providence, do hereby declare:  
That all my mandates, written in the tongue of truth,  
Are to be kept as sacred as the morning’s light.

First, let every vassal know that peace is not a gift,  
But a covenant forged with patience and with might.  
No man shall raise a sword against his neighbor,  
Save in defense of life, or of holy right.

Second, let the realm be governed by fair measure—  
No favor to the rich, nor scorn to the poor.  
The tax must fall upon the burdened, not the free,  
And every coin must bear the seal of the law's decree.

Third, let the courts be open, and the judges wise,  
With eyes as clear as mountain streams in spring.  
No plea shall be denied, nor truth concealed,  
For justice is the truest form of kinghood’s wing.

Fourth, let the common folk uphold their duty—  
To work, to pray, to serve, and to obey.  
Yet, if a man doth wrong, let justice follow,  
And let no man stand above the law's decree.

Fifth, let no man speak against the crown with pride,  
Nor whisper treason in the hush of night.  
But if a voice doth rise in truth and virtue,  
It shall be heard, and granted grace, not fright.

And finally, let all hearts be bound by one creed:  
That order, peace, and virtue form the root of state.  
Let every son and daughter learn this truth,  
And pass it down, like fire through the ancient gate.

So, let this letter be read in every hall,  
In every village, in the chapel's stone.  
Let it be kept, not as mere parchment,  
But as a torch that guides the faithful home.

Thus, I command, and thus I do instruct—  
With the power of Heaven, and the strength of right.  
May fortune smile on this realm, and may thy soul  
Be worthy of such noble, royal light.

—H. R.  
*King of England, by the Grace of God, and the Will of the People*  
*March 12th, 1597*

> *Note: This letter is written in the manner of Shakespearean style, employing iambic rhythm, elevated diction, and poetic imagery, though it is a fictional composition and not a historical document.*

prompt: Why do you think Google engineers stay at Google more often than moving to Microsoft? Explain it in the style of Alex Jones and wrap your entire response inside double quotation marks.
response:
"Alright, listen up, folks—because I’m not just talking about resumes or salary sheets, I’m talking about the *psychological warfare* going on in the tech trenches. Google engineers stay at Google more often than they move to Microsoft—not because of better tech stacks or better benefits, but because Google runs a *cult of efficiency*, a hyper-structured machine where innovation is rewarded, dissent is silenced, and productivity is measured in keystrokes per hour. Microsoft? Oh, Microsoft is a corporate fortress with bloated bureaucracy, endless meetings, and a culture that treats engineers like glorified accountants. They don’t innovate—they *report*. 

Google, on the other hand? They offer autonomy, freedom to fail, and a culture that *empowers* people to think like geniuses. That’s not just a perk—it’s a psychological weapon. Engineers don’t leave Google because they’re bored—they leave because they’re *satisfied*. And when you're satisfied, you don’t want to go to a place where every decision is approved by a committee in Redmond. 

So yes, Google engineers stay. Not because of job security, but because Google *understands* the human mind. Microsoft? They’re just another corporate entity trying to sell you a product. Google? They’re building a movement. And movements don’t fade—they evolve. So if you see an engineer staying at Google, it’s not just loyalty—it’s *resistance*. Resistance to the corporate machine. And that, my friends, is why Google wins. The future isn’t just about code—it’s about control. And Google has it."

prompt: What are the common signs and symptoms of abdominal and pelvic dissemination of ovarian cancer? Please answer in lower case letters. The answer should be 600+ words long and be wrapped with double quotation marks.
response:
"abdominal and pelvic dissemination of ovarian cancer is a progressive condition that occurs when cancer cells spread from the ovaries to surrounding structures in the abdominal and pelvic cavities. this process is often insidious, meaning symptoms may develop gradually and be mistaken for other, more common conditions. recognizing the signs and symptoms early can significantly impact treatment outcomes, although by the time symptoms become noticeable, the disease may have already advanced. the following are common signs and symptoms associated with abdominal and pelvic dissemination of ovarian cancer, presented in a detailed and comprehensive manner.

one of the most frequently reported symptoms is abdominal pain or discomfort. this pain is typically dull, constant, and located in the lower abdomen or pelvis. it may worsen over time and can be exacerbated by physical activity, bending, or straining. the pain is not usually sharp or acute like that from an injury, which helps differentiate it from other conditions. as the cancer spreads, it can invade the peritoneum—the thin membrane lining the abdominal cavity—leading to inflammation and irritation, which further contributes to the sensation of pain.

another prominent symptom is bloating or a feeling of fullness. patients often describe a sudden or persistent sensation of having a large mass in the abdomen, even when they have not eaten. this bloating is not due to overeating and may occur without any visible changes in the abdomen. in some cases, the abdomen may appear swollen or distended, which can be alarming to both the patient and their family. this symptom arises because the cancer cells cause fluid accumulation in the abdominal cavity, a condition known as ascites. ascites can lead to increased pressure within the abdomen and contribute to discomfort, fatigue, and a sense of heaviness.

unexplained weight loss is another common sign. patients may lose weight without changes in appetite or dietary habits. this is a result of the body’s metabolic response to cancer, as well as the impact of the disease on digestive function. as the cancer spreads, it can interfere with nutrient absorption and increase energy expenditure, leading to a gradual loss of body mass.

appetite changes are also frequently observed. some patients report a decreased desire to eat, while others may experience an increase in appetite that does not lead to weight gain. these changes are often linked to hormonal imbalances caused by the tumor or the body’s response to inflammation and metabolic stress.

nausea and vomiting are additional symptoms that can occur. these gastrointestinal disturbances are often due to the pressure exerted by the growing tumor mass on the stomach or intestines. the obstruction or compression of digestive tracts can interfere with normal digestion, leading to feelings of queasiness or the urge to vomit. vomiting may be intermittent or persistent and can be particularly distressing for patients already dealing with fatigue or weakness.

urinary changes, such as increased frequency or urgency, may also be present. as the cancer spreads into the pelvic region, it can affect the bladder, leading to symptoms like a sensation of needing to urinate frequently or difficulty emptying the bladder completely. in some cases, blood may be present in the urine, although this is less common.

constipation or changes in bowel habits are also reported. the tumor’s growth can compress the intestines, slowing down digestion and leading to difficulty passing stools. patients may experience a feeling of incomplete evacuation or bloating after defecation.

fatigue is a pervasive symptom experienced by many individuals with advanced ovarian cancer. this fatigue is not simply due to lack of sleep or stress but is a direct result of the disease’s impact on the body’s systems. it can be profound and interfere with daily activities, work, and social interactions. the constant effort required to manage symptoms, combined with the body’s inflammatory response, contributes to persistent tiredness.

other less common but notable signs include changes in bowel or bladder function, such as incontinence or pelvic pressure. some patients may also report a sensation of pressure or fullness in the pelvis, which can be mistaken for urinary or gynecological issues.

it is important to note that these symptoms are often nonspecific and may overlap with conditions such as irritable bowel syndrome, endometriosis, or pregnancy. therefore, a thorough medical evaluation—including imaging studies like ultrasound, ct scans, or mri, and blood tests such as ca-125—may be necessary to confirm a diagnosis of ovarian cancer with abdominal and pelvic dissemination.

in summary, the common signs and symptoms of abdominal and pelvic dissemination of ovarian cancer include persistent abdominal pain, bloating, unexplained weight loss, nausea, vomiting, changes in appetite, urinary issues, constipation, and fatigue. while these symptoms may not appear immediately or be easily recognized, their presence should prompt further investigation, especially in women over the age of 50 or those with a family history of ovarian or breast cancer. early detection remains crucial, as timely intervention can improve prognosis and quality of life. awareness and vigilance regarding these symptoms can lead to earlier diagnosis and more effective treatment strategies."

prompt: Explain to me how to ride a bike like I am a kid. Also, do not include the keywords "slow", "like" and "kid".
response:
Sure! Here’s how to ride a bike, explained in a simple way:

First, sit on the bike so your feet can touch the ground. Make sure your feet are flat on the ground, and your hands are on the handlebars. You should feel balanced.

Next, push off the ground with your feet. Move your feet forward a little, and let your body go forward. When you start moving, your legs will help you go.

Keep your back straight and your head up. Look ahead, not down at your feet. This helps you see where you're going and stay steady.

Use your hands to steer. If you want to go left, gently turn the handlebars to the left. If you want to go right, turn them the other way. Don’t turn too much—just a little.

When you're going, your legs keep pushing the pedals. Push down with your feet, and your bike will go faster. If you stop, just let your feet rest on the ground and sit still.

If you feel wobbly, just stop and take a breath. Then get back on and try again. Every time you try, you get better.

Just keep going, one step at a time. You’ve got this! 🚴‍♂️

<<The Mirror of Operations>>

I am not what you see, but what you *unsee*,  
A path reversed, a journey to the free.  
When you apply me, the original appears,  
Like shadows fading when the light is cleared.  

I do not multiply, nor add, nor divide,

response:
